title: Source-aware load-use stalls
motivation:
The current load-use interlock compares a load destination against both
instruction bitfields that look like rs1 and rs2, even when the following
instruction does not architecturally read one or both sources. CoreMark has
dense load and immediate/control sequences, so false one-cycle bubbles can
reduce iterations per cycle without improving correctness.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current load-use interlock compares a load destination against both
instruction bitfields that look like rs1 and rs2, even when the following
instruction does not architecturally read one or both sources. CoreMark has
dense load and immediate/control sequences, so false one-cycle bubbles can
reduce iterations per cycle without improving correctness.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Make the hazard unit source-use aware for the IF/ID instruction. Keep the
existing true load-use interlock, but only compare rs1 for instructions that
actually read rs1 and only compare rs2 for instructions that actually read
rs2. This should remove spurious stalls after loads for OP-IMM, LOAD, JALR,
LUI, AUIPC, JAL, FENCE, and SYSTEM encodings while preserving all real RAW
hazards and the existing RVFI retirement behavior.

Pass the full IF/ID instruction, or equivalent source-use signals, into the hazard unit instead of relying on unconditional rs1/rs2 bitfield comparisons.

title: Move DIV/REM off the ALU critical path
motivation:
The current execute stage contains a fully combinational RV32IM ALU, including
SystemVerilog division and remainder operators in the same result mux as the
common RV32I and MUL operations. On the Tang Nano 20K this likely bloats LUT
usage and lengthens the EX critical path even though CoreMark is dominated by
ordinary integer, load/store, branch, and multiply instructions rather than
divide/remainder.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current execute stage contains a fully combinational RV32IM ALU, including
SystemVerilog division and remainder operators in the same result mux as the
common RV32I and MUL operations. On the Tang Nano 20K this likely bloats LUT
usage and lengthens the EX critical path even though CoreMark is dominated by
ordinary integer, load/store, branch, and multiply instructions rather than
divide/remainder.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Split DIV/DIVU/REM/REMU out of the combinational ALU into a small iterative
multi-cycle M-extension side unit in EX. Keep ADD/SUB/logic/shift/compare and
the MUL-family operations on the existing single-cycle path, but detect divide
and remainder opcodes in EX, start the divider, and stall the pipeline until
the result is available. This structural decoupling should reduce the normal
ALU critical path and LUT pressure with negligible CoreMark CPI cost because
rare DIV/REM instructions pay the added latency only when they occur.

title: Static backward branch predictor
motivation: The current five-stage core resolves all branches and jumps in EX, so every taken loop branch or JAL pays a two-instruction flush penalty. CoreMark has tight loops and frequent backward branches, making a very small front-end predictor likely to improve iterations per cycle without the LUT and Fmax cost of a larger dynamic predictor.

hypothesis.hypothesis: Add a static predict-taken path in IF for aligned backward conditional branches and aligned unconditional JAL, while keeping forward conditional branches predicted not-taken and JALR resolved in EX. Carry the predicted next PC through ID/EX, compare it against the actual EX-resolved next PC, and redirect only on mispredict. This should remove most loop-back branch penalties with only immediate decode/add logic in the fetch path and no predictor RAM state.

title: Collapse ALU multiply products
motivation:
The post-DIV baseline still computes three independent 64-bit multiplier
products in the combinational ALU: unsigned*unsigned, signed*signed, and
signed*unsigned. CoreMark benefits from keeping MUL single-cycle, but the
extra high-half multiplier hardware likely costs LUTs, routing, and Fmax on
the Tang Nano 20K even when only one M-extension result is selected.

hypothesis.motivation:
The post-DIV baseline still computes three independent 64-bit multiplier
products in the combinational ALU: unsigned*unsigned, signed*signed, and
signed*unsigned. CoreMark benefits from keeping MUL single-cycle, but the
extra high-half multiplier hardware likely costs LUTs, routing, and Fmax on
the Tang Nano 20K even when only one M-extension result is selected.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the three parallel ALU multiplier products with one unsigned 32x32
product plus high-half sign-correction subtractors. The low 32 bits are
identical for MUL under any signedness; MULHU uses the raw high half; MULH
subtracts the opposite operand from the high half for each negative input;
and MULHSU subtracts the unsigned multiplier operand when rs1 is negative.
This preserves the existing single-cycle M-extension CPI while reducing
replicated multiplier logic and routing pressure on the ALU result path.

changes.description: Compute one unsigned product and derive MULH/MULHSU high halves with RV32M-correct sign-correction logic instead of instantiating three parallel products.
Add focused MULH and MULHSU edge vectors covering negative operands, INT_MIN, all-ones, and mixed signed/unsigned correction cases.
Compute one unsigned product and derive MULH/MULHSU high halves with RV32M-correct sign-correction logic instead of instantiating three parallel products.

lesson: - 2026-05-12 hyp-20260512-001-r2s0 (regression, -2.76%): Collapsing MULH/MULHU/MULHSU to one unsigned product plus high-half sign-correction subtractors can hurt Tang Nano 20K fitness because the added subtractor chain sits on the selected multiplier result path while parallel inferred multipliers are handled efficiently.

title: Decoupled fetch skid buffer
motivation:
The current front end is tightly coupled to the rest of the pipeline: any
dmem stall, load-use stall, divide stall, or raw imem-not-ready cycle holds
the PC and prevents useful fetch progress. In the tournament CoreMark mode
both instruction and data ports see random backpressure, so exposed fetch
bubbles reduce iterations per cycle even when the instruction port could
have fetched ahead during unrelated back-end stalls.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current front end is tightly coupled to the rest of the pipeline: any
dmem stall, load-use stall, divide stall, or raw imem-not-ready cycle holds
the PC and prevents useful fetch progress. In the tournament CoreMark mode
both instruction and data ports see random backpressure, so exposed fetch
bubbles reduce iterations per cycle even when the instruction port could
have fetched ahead during unrelated back-end stalls.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny two-entry fetch skid buffer between IF and ID. The IF stage should
keep issuing sequential fetches while the decode/back-end side is stalled and
there is queue space, and ID should consume from the queue whenever it can
advance. Redirects still flush the queue and restart at the resolved target,
preserving the current EX-resolution semantics and RVFI ordering. This
structural decoupling should hide a fraction of random imem stalls behind
dmem/load-use/div stalls with modest LUT/FF cost and less timing risk than
putting branch prediction logic back on the fetch critical path.

Base front-end stalls on fetch-queue empty/full status instead of raw imemReady, allowing decode to proceed from buffered instructions during imem backpressure.
Wire the fetch queue status into the existing hazard/control path while preserving the nret=1 RVFI port set and single-retire channel behavior.

title: Tiny BTB branch predictor
motivation:
The current core resolves every taken branch and jump in EX, so tight CoreMark
loops pay a two-instruction redirect penalty on each taken iteration. The prior
static backward predictor showed that avoiding these penalties can help CPI,
but its IF-stage immediate decode and target add hurt Fmax enough to lose most
of the gain.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current core resolves every taken branch and jump in EX, so tight CoreMark
loops pay a two-instruction redirect penalty on each taken iteration. The prior
static backward predictor showed that avoiding these penalties can help CPI,
but its IF-stage immediate decode and target add hurt Fmax enough to lose most
of the gain.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a small direct-mapped BTB with 2-bit saturating counters, updated only from
resolved control-flow instructions in EX. IF predicts taken only on a valid tag
hit with a taken counter and uses the stored target, otherwise it fetches pc+4;
this keeps branch immediate decode and target addition out of the fetch path.
Carry predicted next-PC metadata through IF/ID and ID/EX so EX redirects only
when the stored prediction disagrees with the actual resolved next PC. This
should recover most repeated loop-branch and unconditional jump penalties after
warm-up with lower Fmax risk than the failed static predictor.

title: Source-aware load-use interlock
motivation: The current load-use hazard compares the prior load destination against both raw rs1 and rs2 bitfields of the following instruction. For many RV32I encodings one or both fields are not architecturally read, so the core can inject a one-cycle bubble after loads even when there is no true dependency. CoreMark has dense load, immediate, address-update, and control sequences, so eliminating false bubbles should improve iterations per cycle with very small RTL cost.

hypothesis.motivation: The current load-use hazard compares the prior load destination against both raw rs1 and rs2 bitfields of the following instruction. For many RV32I encodings one or both fields are not architecturally read, so the core can inject a one-cycle bubble after loads even when there is no true dependency. CoreMark has dense load, immediate, address-update, and control sequences, so eliminating false bubbles should improve iterations per cycle with very small RTL cost.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Add compact IF/ID source-use predicates and gate the existing load-use comparison with them. Instructions that only read rs1, only read rs2, or read no integer source should not stall just because an immediate or destination bitfield happens to equal the load rd. The implementation must leave the raw instruction rs bitfields and regfile-read values unchanged through the pipeline so RVFI and cosim still see the same architectural source metadata; only the interlock predicate changes.

title: Register the IF/ID boundary
motivation: The current front end has no real IF/ID register: io_imemData feeds decode, immediate generation, regfile address selection, hazard inputs, and the ID/EX register in the same cycle. That structure keeps CPI low but likely puts external instruction-memory output, decoder fanout, and regfile read timing on one FPGA-critical path. Prior fetch-ahead structures lost badly because queue control and prediction logic made the front end more complex; a single registered IF/ID boundary is the minimal structural retiming that targets the same timing path without adding speculation or buffering policy.

Prior fetch-ahead structures lost badly because queue control and prediction logic made the front end more complex; a single registered IF/ID boundary is the minimal structural retiming that targets the same timing path without adding speculation or buffering policy.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Convert if_stage from a combinational IF/ID payload producer into a simple one-entry registered fetch/decode boundary. The PC still advances sequentially when the front end is not stalled, redirects still override stalls and flush the younger instruction, and ID consumes the registered instruction exactly as today. This should break the imem_data-to-decoder-to-ID/EX critical path and improve Fmax while preserving single-issue throughput; the expected CPI cost is limited to unchanged redirect/load-use behavior rather than the extra queue-control overhead seen in the failed skid-buffer attempt.

title: ID-stage static control predictor
motivation:
The current core resolves every taken branch and jump in EX, so CoreMark loop
backs and direct jumps pay a two-instruction wrong-path flush. Prior IF-stage
static and BTB predictors showed that eliminating this penalty can help CPI,
but putting immediate decode, target add, tag compare, or predictor RAM lookup
on the fetch next-PC path costs too much Fmax on Tang Nano 20K.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current core resolves every taken branch and jump in EX, so CoreMark loop
backs and direct jumps pay a two-instruction wrong-path flush. Prior IF-stage
static and BTB predictors showed that eliminating this penalty can help CPI,
but putting immediate decode, target add, tag compare, or predictor RAM lookup
on the fetch next-PC path costs too much Fmax on Tang Nano 20K.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Move the lightweight static predictor out of IF and into ID. IF should remain
a simple sequential pc+4 fetch unless an existing redirect arrives, while ID
decodes the fetched instruction and redirects one stage early for aligned JAL
and aligned backward conditional branches. Carry the ID-predicted next PC into
EX so the branch/jump resolver redirects only when the actual next PC differs
from the prediction. This keeps predictor work off the front-end critical path
and still saves one of the two taken-control-flow bubbles in CoreMark loops.

title: Case-based MEM byte-lane muxes
motivation:
The current best core already gained by removing rare DIV/REM from the common
EX datapath, while recent front-end and multiplier micro-optimizations lost
Fmax. A lower-risk remaining hot path is the MEM/WB load-data and store-mask
logic: CoreMark is load/store heavy, and the current MEM stage uses variable
shifts for byte extraction and byte-lane mask generation in the same cycle
that load data can feed writeback and forwarding.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current best core already gained by removing rare DIV/REM from the common
EX datapath, while recent front-end and multiplier micro-optimizations lost
Fmax. A lower-risk remaining hot path is the MEM/WB load-data and store-mask
logic: CoreMark is load/store heavy, and the current MEM stage uses variable
shifts for byte extraction and byte-lane mask generation in the same cycle
that load data can feed writeback and forwarding.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the MEM-stage variable shifts with explicit case muxes indexed by
address bits. Generate byte and halfword load slices directly from
dmem_rdata[7:0], [15:8], [23:16], and [31:24], and generate store masks from
width/address case arms rather than left-shifting a constant. This should map
to smaller LUT mux trees on Tang Nano 20K, shorten the dmem_rdata-to-WB and
address-to-mask paths, and preserve the exact RVFI aligned-memory reporting,
misalignment traps, and pipeline timing.

title: Iterative high-half multiply side unit
motivation:
The successful DIV/REM side-unit hypothesis showed that this FPGA benefits
when rare, wide arithmetic is removed from the common EX-stage ALU result
path. The current ALU still computes three 64-bit multiply variants every
cycle and muxes their high halves alongside the common RV32I and low-half
MUL operations. CoreMark should mostly exercise ordinary integer ops and
low-half MUL, while MULH/MULHU/MULHSU are uncommon in compiler output.

hypothesis.motivation:

path. The current ALU still computes three 64-bit multiply variants every
cycle and muxes their high halves alongside the common RV32I and low-half
MUL operations. CoreMark should mostly exercise ordinary integer ops and
low-half MUL, while MULH/MULHU/MULHSU are uncommon in compiler output.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Keep ALU_MUL as a single-cycle low-half multiply, but move ALU_MULH,
ALU_MULHU, and ALU_MULHSU into a small multi-cycle high-multiply side unit
owned by EX, using the same stall/consume pattern already proven for DIV/REM.
The common ALU path then only needs the low-half unsigned product for MUL and
no longer carries signed high-product result muxing on every instruction.
Rare high-half multiply instructions pay a short structural stall, but the
expected CoreMark mix should recover more Fmax than it loses in IPC.

title: Single-entry loop target predictor
motivation: The failed predictor attempts show that broad prediction logic loses when it adds immediate decode, target adders, RAM lookup, counters, or same-cycle ID redirect control to the front-end critical path. CoreMark still has repeated loop-back branches that pay the current EX-resolution flush penalty, so the best remaining predictor angle is a much narrower mechanism that only learns the last hot backward branch target and keeps all target computation in EX.

CoreMark still has repeated loop-back branches that pay the current EX-resolution flush penalty, so the best remaining predictor angle is a much narrower mechanism that only learns the last hot backward branch target and keeps all target computation in EX.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Add a single-entry last-taken backward-branch predictor updated from resolved EX-stage branches. IF predicts taken only when the current PC exactly matches the stored branch PC, using the already-registered target from the entry; all other fetches stay simple pc+4, and no instruction immediate decode or branch target addition occurs in IF. Carry the predicted next PC through IF/ID and ID/EX so EX redirects only when the actual resolved next PC differs, correcting loop exits and cold misses while eliminating most repeat loop-back bubbles with far less timing cost than the prior static and BTB predictors.

title: Drop regfile bulk reset
motivation:
The current core still synchronously clears all 32 integer registers on reset.
That adds reset fanout and reset muxing across roughly a thousand register-file
bits, even though RV32I does not architecturally require x1-x31 to reset to
zero and benchmark software initializes the registers it depends on. Prior
micro-opt attempts that touched fetch prediction or multiplier structure paid
control/datapath costs, so a contained timing cleanup with no CPI effect is a
better fit for the next slot.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Remove the bulk reset loop from the integer register file while preserving x0
hardwiring, normal synchronous writes, and the same-cycle write-first read
bypass. This should reduce reset-net fanout and per-bit reset control in a hot
always-on structure, giving nextpnr more freedom to place the regfile and its
bypass muxes. CoreMark CPI should be unchanged because the pipeline behavior,
forwarding, RVFI retirement, and writeback timing are identical after reset.

changes.description: Remove the synchronous reset clearing loop for x1-x31, keep x0 reads forced to zero, and preserve the write-first bypass and write gating semantics.

lesson: - 2026-05-13 hyp-20260513-001-r5s0 (regression, -0.24%): Removing the regfile bulk reset did not improve Tang Nano 20K fitness, suggesting reset fanout/muxing on x1-x31 is not the binding path compared with the core's active decode, memory, and ALU datapaths.

title: Selective store stall bypass
motivation: The current pipeline freezes globally whenever a STORE reaches MEM while io_dmemReady is low. In the tournament CoreMark mode the data port has random backpressure, so store stalls unnecessarily block later independent ALU, branch, and multiply work even though the store payload is already fully known at MEM. Prior front-end decoupling attempts regressed because they added timing-critical fetch control; a back-end-only decoupling point should target IPC under dStall with less fetch-path risk.

hypothesis.motivation: The current pipeline freezes globally whenever a STORE reaches MEM while io_dmemReady is low. Prior front-end decoupling attempts regressed because they added timing-critical fetch control; a back-end-only decoupling point should target IPC under dStall with less fetch-path risk.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Add a one-entry store buffer at the MEM boundary for aligned, non-MMIO stores. When a normal store reaches MEM and dmem is not ready, capture its address, replicated write data, and byte mask, allow the store to retire through MEM/WB, and keep driving the buffered write until the bus accepts it. While the buffer is occupied, stall only younger LOAD/STORE instructions to preserve memory ordering and the single dmem port; let younger non-memory instructions continue. Do not buffer UART or timing-marker MMIO stores so CoreMark brackets and output ordering remain exact. This should hide a fraction of random store backpressure cycles without changing nret, branch behavior, or common EX timing.

changes.description: Add a one-entry non-MMIO store buffer, drive buffered writes on the dmem port until accepted, and retire buffered stores through MEM/WB without double-retirement.

Replace the current all-memory dmem stall policy with store-buffer-aware stalls that block loads/stores when ordering or buffer capacity requires it but allow younger non-memory operations to proceed.
Wire store-buffer status and separate EX/MEM load/store control into the hazard unit while preserving the nret=1 RVFI port contract.

title: Tiny JALR return target predictor
motivation:
Prior branch predictor attempts lost fitness because they put immediate decode,
target addition, BTB lookup, or loop-PC compare control on the front-end timing
path. CoreMark still pays the existing EX-resolution flush on direct calls,
indirect returns, and other taken JALR instructions, and return targets are often
stable for a given return instruction PC in this small benchmark binary.

hypothesis.motivation:
Prior branch predictor attempts lost fitness because they put immediate decode,
target addition, BTB lookup, or loop-PC compare control on the front-end timing
path. CoreMark still pays the existing EX-resolution flush on direct calls,
indirect returns, and other taken JALR instructions, and return targets are often
stable for a given return instruction PC in this small benchmark binary.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a very narrow return-target predictor updated only from resolved EX-stage
JALR return-like instructions, such as JALR x0, x1/x5, 0. Keep target computation
in EX and store the resolved return instruction PC plus actual target in a tiny
one-entry or two-entry table. IF remains mostly sequential and predicts only on
an exact registered PC hit, using the stored target with no instruction decode,
immediate generation, or target add in the fetch path. Carry the predicted next
PC through ID/EX so EX corrects stale entries and non-return JALR cases. This
targets a different CoreMark control-flow penalty than the failed loop predictors
while keeping the predictor small enough to have a plausible Fmax/IPC tradeoff.

Add a tiny registered return-target cache lookup by fetch PC and select the stored target only on an exact hit, leaving the default path as pc+4.

title: Prune late pipeline control fields
motivation: Recent attempts show that adding front-end prediction or arithmetic side-control tends to cost more Fmax than it saves, while the successful MEM-stage case-mux change improved fitness by simplifying always-active datapaths. The current pipeline still carries the full decoded ctrl_t through EX/MEM and MEM/WB even though many fields are dead after EX, and almost all fields are dead after MEM. Those unnecessary bits add flops, reset/hold muxing, struct routing, and fanout on hot writeback and forwarding control.

hypothesis.motivation: Recent attempts show that adding front-end prediction or arithmetic side-control tends to cost more Fmax than it saves, while the successful MEM-stage case-mux change improved fitness by simplifying always-active datapaths. The current pipeline still carries the full decoded ctrl_t through EX/MEM and MEM/WB even though many fields are dead after EX, and almost all fields are dead after MEM. Those unnecessary bits add flops, reset/hold muxing, struct routing, and fanout on hot writeback and forwarding control.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Split the late pipeline control payload into narrower MEM-stage and WB-stage control structs. EX/MEM only needs memory access width/sign, mem read/write, reg_write, mem_to_reg, and trap state; MEM/WB only needs reg_write, mem_to_reg, and trap state because RVFI memory masks/data have already been materialized. Removing unused branch, jump, ALU-op, operand-select, AUIPC/LUI, and memory-format bits from late pipeline registers should reduce Tang Nano 20K placement/routing pressure and shorten small but always-active control muxes without changing instruction timing, RVFI fields, or CoreMark CPI.

title: MEM load-data bypass to EX
motivation:
The current five-stage pipeline always injects a bubble for a load-use pair,
even when the data memory is ready and the load data is available during the
following MEM cycle. CoreMark is load/store heavy, and the recent MEM-stage
byte-lane improvement indicates this benchmark is sensitive to the memory
datapath. Prior structural frontend decoupling and prediction attempts paid
too much fetch-path timing cost, so a backend bypass is a more focused IPC
improvement.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current five-stage pipeline always injects a bubble for a load-use pair,
even when the data memory is ready and the load data is available during the
following MEM cycle. CoreMark is load/store heavy, and the recent MEM-stage
byte-lane improvement indicates this benchmark is sensitive to the memory
datapath. Prior structural frontend decoupling and prediction attempts paid
too much fetch-path timing cost, so a backend bypass is a more focused IPC

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a direct MEM-stage load-result bypass into the EX operand forwarding
network. When a load sits in EX/MEM and a younger instruction in ID/EX reads
the same rd, EX should use the current MEM load_data instead of stalling for
the value to reach MEM/WB. The bypass must be disabled for misaligned/trapping
loads so dependent instructions retain the old architectural register value,
and dmem backpressure should still freeze the pipeline until valid load data
is available. This removes the normal one-cycle load-use penalty without
adding branch prediction, fetch queues, or nret changes.

Add a MEM-load forwarding input and select it for rs1/rs2 operands ahead of the older MEM/WB path.
Teach forwarding to distinguish EX/MEM ALU-result forwarding from EX/MEM load-data bypass forwarding.
Remove the unconditional load-use bubble when the new MEM-to-EX bypass can safely satisfy the dependency, while preserving stalls under dmem backpressure.
Wire the MEM-stage bypass value and valid signal into the forwarding and EX-stage operand paths.

title: Lookahead hot-branch predictor
motivation: Prior predictor attempts lost because the prediction decision sat directly on the current IF next-PC path: immediate decode, target adders, tag compares, or same-cycle ID redirects reduced Fmax more than CoreMark gained from fewer taken-branch bubbles. CoreMark still has hot loop-back branches, so the remaining promising predictor angle is to keep prediction state registered and move the lookup one cycle ahead of the branch fetch.

hypothesis.motivation: Prior predictor attempts lost because the prediction decision sat directly on the current IF next-PC path: immediate decode, target adders, tag compares, or same-cycle ID redirects reduced Fmax more than CoreMark gained from fewer taken-branch bubbles. CoreMark still has hot loop-back branches, so the remaining promising predictor angle is to keep prediction state registered and move the lookup one cycle ahead of the branch fetch.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Add a one-entry hot control-flow predictor that is updated from EX with the resolved PC and target of taken backward branches and direct jumps, but consume it through a one-cycle sequential lookahead. While fetching PC N, compare the registered predictor PC against N+4 and latch a prearmed prediction for the next sequential fetch; when PC N+4 is fetched on the following cycle, the IF next-PC mux uses only the registered prearmed target, not a same-cycle tag compare or target adder. Carry the predicted next PC with the instruction so EX redirects only on a real mismatch. This should recover loop-back taken-branch bubbles while avoiding the timing pattern that broke the prior IF BTB, static, loop, and return predictors.

Wire prediction metadata and EX predictor-update signals between IF, ID, and EX while preserving the nret=1 RVFI port contract.

title: Drop dead branch payload
motivation:
Recent micro-optimization attempts show that added front-end logic, broad
control repacking, and multiplier restructuring tend to lose Fmax, while
targeted removal or simplification of always-carried datapath logic is the
safer path. In the current bench core, branch resolution is fully completed
inside EX: redirect, predictor update, pc_next, and trap handling are all
produced before the EX/MEM register. The EX/MEM payload still carries
branch_taken and branch_target even though MEM, WB, RVFI, forwarding, and
hazard logic do not consume them.

hypothesis.motivation:
Recent micro-optimization attempts show that added front-end logic, broad
control repacking, and multiplier restructuring tend to lose Fmax, while
targeted removal or simplification of always-carried datapath logic is the

branch_taken and branch_target even though MEM, WB, RVFI, forwarding, and
hazard logic do not consume them.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Remove the unused branch_taken and branch_target fields from ex_mem_t and
delete the corresponding EX-stage register assignments. This should reduce
the EX/MEM register by 33 bits, including reset and dmem-stall hold muxing,
without changing instruction timing, branch redirect behavior, predictor
updates, RVFI pc_wdata, trap behavior, or CoreMark CPI. The change is much
narrower than the prior late-control pruning attempt: it only deletes fields
already proven dead downstream, avoiding new packed-control structs or extra
muxing on hot paths.

title: Precompute MEM access descriptor in EX
motivation: The strongest recent non-predictor gain came from simplifying MEM-stage byte-lane muxing, which suggests the load/store formatting path is still important for Tang Nano 20K fitness. The current MEM stage still derives misalignment, byte masks, replicated store data, aligned RVFI addresses, and dmem enables in the same cycle that dmem_rdata is selected and forwarded to writeback. Front-end queues, IF retiming, high-multiply side units, and direct load-data bypassing have already shown poor tradeoffs, so the next structural target should be a no-CPI retiming of an existing hot back-end path.

hypothesis.motivation: The strongest recent non-predictor gain came from simplifying MEM-stage byte-lane muxing, which suggests the load/store formatting path is still important for Tang Nano 20K fitness. The current MEM stage still derives misalignment, byte masks, replicated store data, aligned RVFI addresses, and dmem enables in the same cycle that dmem_rdata is selected and forwarded to writeback. Front-end queues, IF retiming, high-multiply side units, and direct load-data bypassing have already shown poor tradeoffs, so the next structural target should be a no-CPI retiming of an existing hot back-end path.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Move the address-dependent memory access descriptor generation from MEM into EX, immediately after the effective address is computed. EX should precompute the byte mask, replicated store data, word-aligned RVFI address, memory-misalignment trap bit, and trap-gated control for load/store instructions, then carry those registered fields in EX/MEM. MEM can then drive dmem_wen/dmem_ren, RVFI masks, and store write data mostly from registered descriptor fields while retaining the existing load-data extraction from dmem_rdata. This should shorten the MEM address/control-to-writeback and dmem-enable paths with no CPI cost, while adding only small EX-side logic based on alu_result[1:0] and existing memory control bits.

title: Two-entry lookahead hot predictor
motivation: The current one-entry lookahead predictor is the first predictor variant that improved fitness because it keeps the current IF next-PC mux free of tag comparisons and target adders. CoreMark still contains nested loops and direct control-flow edges, so a single learned PC can be evicted by another hot edge and lose most of the warm predictor benefit between repeated visits.

hypothesis.motivation: The current one-entry lookahead predictor is the first predictor variant that improved fitness because it keeps the current IF next-PC mux free of tag comparisons and target adders. CoreMark still contains nested loops and direct control-flow edges, so a single learned PC can be evicted by another hot edge and lose most of the warm predictor benefit between repeated visits.
hypothesis.hypothesis: Expand the existing registered lookahead predictor in if_stage from one entry to two small entries, updated from the same EX-resolved taken backward branches and direct jumps. While fetching PC N, compare N+4 against both registered entry PCs only to prearm the following cycle; when the predicted instruction is actually fetched, the next-PC mux still consumes only the already-registered armed target. This should reduce predictor thrashing on nested CoreMark loops and call/jump structure without reintroducing the timing pattern that broke current-PC BTBs, static IF decode, ID redirects, or return predictors.

changes.description: Replace the single pred_valid/pred_pc/pred_target entry with two registered entries, add simple replacement on EX updates, and prearm from a two-entry lookahead match while keeping the current next-PC mux driven only by armed_valid_q and armed_target_q.

lesson: - 2026-05-13 hyp-20260513-003-r7s2 (regression, -22.20%): Expanding the registered lookahead predictor from one to two entries can lose because extra seq_pc tag compares, target priority muxing, and replacement state add IF arming complexity beyond CoreMark's remaining hot-edge thrash benefit.

title: Filter hot predictor JAL pollution
motivation:
The current best core gained from a one-entry lookahead hot-branch predictor
because the fetch next-PC path consumes only registered prearmed state. The
remaining risk is that the single entry is updated by every direct JAL as
well as hot backward loop branches, so forward calls in CoreMark can evict
the loop-back edge that the predictor is best suited to accelerate.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current best core gained from a one-entry lookahead hot-branch predictor
because the fetch next-PC path consumes only registered prearmed state. The
remaining risk is that the single entry is updated by every direct JAL as
well as hot backward loop branches, so forward calls in CoreMark can evict
the loop-back edge that the predictor is best suited to accelerate.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Narrow the existing predictor update policy so the one-entry lookahead slot
learns only taken backward control-flow edges, primarily conditional loop
branches and any rare backward JALs, while ignoring forward direct-call JALs.
This keeps the successful registered lookahead datapath intact, avoids the
two-entry predictor's added compare/replacement cost, and should improve
CoreMark iterations per cycle by keeping the single predictor entry focused
on the hottest loop edge rather than call-site pollution.

changes.description: Change pred_update_valid so the hot lookahead predictor updates only for backward resolved targets, filtering forward JAL call targets while preserving EX correction and RVFI pc_next behavior.

lesson: - 2026-05-13 hyp-20260513-001-r8s0 (regression, -53.91%): Forward direct JAL updates are not mere pollution for the one-entry registered lookahead predictor; filtering it to only backward targets can erase useful CoreMark call-site prediction and badly reduce fitness.

title: Precompute PC-relative targets in ID
motivation: The current best core already benefits from a registered lookahead hot-branch predictor, but EX still recomputes pc+4 and pc+imm for branches, JAL, AUIPC, link writeback, redirect targets, prediction correction, and RVFI pc_wdata. Prior IF-side prediction and buffering attempts regressed because they put extra compares, target adds, or queue control on the fetch PC path. A narrower structural retime can instead move PC-relative adders behind the existing ID/EX register and shorten the EX resolve/writeback muxing without changing speculation policy.

hypothesis.hypothesis: Add registered pc_plus4 and pc_plus_imm fields to the ID/EX payload, computed in id_stage from the fetched PC and decoded immediate. In ex_stage, use those registered values for branch targets, JAL targets, JAL/JALR link data, normal sequential pc_next, and AUIPC writeback, while keeping JALR's rs1+imm calculation in EX because it depends on forwarded rs1. This should reduce the always-active EX pc-add and branch/jump result mux depth while preserving the single-issue nret=1 RVFI contract, existing hot-branch predictor behavior, and all memory/divide stall rules.

title: Loop-only lookahead training
motivation:
The current best predictor is the one-entry sequential lookahead design because
it keeps tag comparison off the current IF next-PC mux. The failed two-entry
expansion suggests the next predictor gain should not add more IF arming
compares or replacement state. A simpler way to reduce one-entry thrash is to
stop training it with direct JAL targets that may be cold calls or one-shot
control transfers and can evict the hot CoreMark loop-back branch.

hypothesis.motivation:
The current best predictor is the one-entry sequential lookahead design because
it keeps tag comparison off the current IF next-PC mux. The failed two-entry
expansion suggests the next predictor gain should not add more IF arming
compares or replacement state. A simpler way to reduce one-entry thrash is to
stop training it with direct JAL targets that may be cold calls or one-shot
control transfers and can evict the hot CoreMark loop-back branch.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Restrict the existing lookahead predictor update to taken backward conditional
branches only, leaving the IF-side predictor entry, lookahead compare, armed
target mux, and carried predicted_pc_next metadata unchanged. This preserves
the timing shape that improved fitness while making the single predictor entry
less prone to pollution from direct jumps. The tradeoff is giving up some JAL
predictions, but CoreMark's most valuable repeated control-flow edges should
be loop branches, so the hotter entry should improve iterations per cycle with
little or no Fmax cost.

changes.description: Narrow pred_update_valid so the one-entry lookahead predictor trains only on valid, legal, taken backward conditional branches instead of also replacing the entry with direct JAL targets.

title: Trim halfword load muxes
motivation:
The best successful micro-optimization so far simplified the MEM-stage
byte-lane datapath by replacing variable shifts with explicit muxes. The
current MEM stage still spends LUT depth on halfword byte lanes that are
architecturally unreachable for legal LH/LHU/SH accesses: addr[0]=1 is a
misalignment trap, dmem is gated off, reg_write is cleared, and the computed

title: Move all MUL ops to a side unit
motivation:
The successful DIV/REM side unit showed that this core benefits when rare or
heavy RV32M arithmetic is removed from the always-active EX ALU result path.
A later high-half-multiply side unit regressed because low-half MUL still left
a 32x32 multiplier in the common ALU mux, so the structural timing bottleneck
was not actually removed while new stall control was added.

The remaining untried structural split is to move the entire MUL family,
including ALU_MUL, out of alu.sv. CoreMark will pay extra cycles on multiply
instructions, but the normal integer, branch, load/store, and predictor paths
should lose the parallel signed/unsigned multiplier fanout and wide M-result
muxing that currently sit in the EX stage.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a dedicated multi-cycle multiply side unit for MUL, MULH, MULHU, and
MULHSU, using the same start/wait/consume pattern as the existing DIV/REM
unit. In normal builds, alu.sv should no longer implement real multiply
datapaths; EX detects multiply opcodes, starts the side unit, emits bubbles
while the result is pending, and writes the side-unit result into EX/MEM when
complete. Under RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS, keep the existing algebraic ALTOPS
substitutions so the fast formal contract remains unchanged.

from the common ALU result path, not just the high-half cases. The risk is
CoreMark CPI loss if MUL is frequent, so the unit should be low-latency and
preferably registered around 16x16 partial products rather than a long
shift-add loop.

title: Promote repeated lookahead targets
motivation: The one-entry registered lookahead predictor is the only predictor variant that improved fitness because IF consumes only prearmed registered state. Later attempts regressed when they added a second IF compare/replacement path or filtered out direct JAL updates entirely, which suggests the entry needs better update selectivity without changing the fetch critical path.

hypothesis.hypothesis: Keep the existing active one-entry lookahead predictor and its single seq_pc compare, but add a non-fetch-path candidate entry used only by the EX update logic. A newly resolved taken backward branch or direct JAL first records as the candidate; it replaces the active predictor only when the same PC is seen again, while updates to the current active PC still refresh its target immediately. This should reduce eviction by one-shot calls and cold control-flow edges while still allowing repeated JALs and nested-loop branches to become predicted, avoiding both the failed two-entry IF lookup and the failed all-JAL filtering policies.

title: Use immediate sign for predictor update
motivation:
The current best core depends on the one-entry registered lookahead predictor,
but recent predictor variants regressed when they added IF-side compares,
replacement state, or delayed promotion. A lower-risk micro-optimization is to
keep the same single-entry predictor structure and useful direct-JAL training,
while trimming EX-stage predictor-update control that is not architecturally
visible.

hypothesis.hypothesis:

bounded CoreMark address range, a negative B-immediate is exactly the loop-back

preserves direct JAL updates that prior results showed are useful, and should
improve Fmax without changing CPI on correctly predicted hot loops.

title: Store-drain buffer for dStall
motivation: The current pipeline globally freezes when a store reaches MEM while dmem is not ready, even though the store address, data, mask, and RVFI payload are already known. CoreMark runs with randomized data-memory backpressure, so store stalls can expose dead cycles that younger independent ALU and branch instructions could otherwise use. Prior structural front-end and arithmetic retiming attempts usually lost Fmax, so the most promising structural angle is a back-end decoupling point that leaves IF, prediction, decode, and the common EX ALU path unchanged.

hypothesis.hypothesis: Add a single-entry store-drain buffer after MEM for aligned, non-MMIO stores. When such a store cannot be accepted by dmem, capture its addr/wdata/wmask, allow the store to advance to MEM/WB and retire once, and keep driving the buffered write until io_dmemReady accepts it. While the buffer is pending, stall younger LOAD/STORE instructions and any MMIO/timing-marker store, but allow younger non-memory instructions to pass through MEM so random store backpressure is partly hidden without changing nret or RVFI order. This is a refined version of the earlier broken store-buffer idea: keep memory ordering conservative, do not buffer UART or CoreMark marker writes, and do not add load forwarding from the buffer.

Wire store-buffer pending/block status between MEM and hazard control without changing the nret=1 RVFI port set or existing predictor interfaces.

title: Directionless hot-edge training
motivation:
The current one-entry registered lookahead predictor is the only predictor variant
that improved fitness because it keeps tag comparison off the current IF next-PC
mux. Later predictor regressions came from adding IF lookup complexity or delaying
useful updates. A narrower update-policy change can try to recover more CoreMark
control-flow bubbles while preserving the successful IF timing shape.

title: Explicit signed compare datapath
motivation: The current best core already gained from simplifying the MEM byte-lane datapath, while most attempts that added predictor state, new side units, or broad control repacking regressed. A remaining low-risk hot cone is signed comparison in EX: SLT and BLT/BGE currently rely on generic signed less-than operators, which can infer full-width comparator/subtractor logic on the ALU and branch-resolution paths.

title: Compact EX branch comparator unit
motivation:
The current best core already recovered useful CoreMark IPC with the registered
hot-edge predictor, so the next structural opportunity should preserve that
behavior while reducing the EX redirect path. ex_stage currently builds branch
equality, signed less-than, unsigned less-than, greater/equal variants, target
selection, misalignment handling, and ALU result muxing in one large block.
Prior front-end queues, IF/ID retiming, store buffers, and multiplier side
units mostly lost because they added control or CPI cost; a local EX compare
reshaping has no new stalls and avoids touching the fetch predictor policy.

when they added IF lookup entries, delayed useful updates, or polluted the
successful update policy. The remaining predictor opportunity is therefore to
keep the IF predictor behavior unchanged while trimming the EX-stage
prediction validation logic that now sits on every valid instruction.

title: Suppress load EX forwarding
motivation: Prior micro-opt results show that this core rewards small removals from always-active datapaths, while broader control repacking and comparator rewrites tend to perturb timing badly. The EX operand forwarding unit still treats a load in EX/MEM as a normal producer even though its architectural rd value is not available until MEM/WB and the existing load-use interlock already prevents a dependent instruction from consuming EX/MEM load data.

title: Register writeback result
motivation:
The current MEM/WB stage still computes the final writeback value with a
combinational mem_to_reg mux in wb_stage. That mux feeds both the regfile
write port and the MEM/WB-to-EX forwarding path, so dependent CoreMark
instructions can see an active path from the MEM/WB register through the
writeback mux, EX operand mux, ALU, and EX/MEM register. Prior structural
attempts that changed fetch policy, store buffering, branch factoring, or
M-extension latency often paid control or CPI costs; this is a narrower
retiming of an existing datapath with no new stalls.

title: Redirect-target predictor prearm
motivation:
The only predictor variant that improved fitness is the current one-entry
registered lookahead predictor: it compares the stored hot PC against pc+4
one cycle early, then the fetch next-PC mux consumes only registered armed
state. Later predictor attempts regressed when they added IF lookup entries,
delayed promotion, changed the update policy, or added carried validation
bits. A remaining gap is that lookahead arming only happens along sequential
fetch. When an EX redirect lands directly on a previously learned hot control
instruction, the predictor cannot be armed because the predecessor PC was not
fetched sequentially.

title: Share EX PC adders
motivation:
Recent micro-opt attempts show that this core is sensitive to added control
and pipeline repacking, while the successful changes were local datapath
simplifications. The EX stage currently spells out the same PC-relative
additions in several places for branch targets, direct JAL targets,
sequential pc_next, and JAL/JALR link data. If synthesis does not fully
common those expressions across procedural blocks, it can leave extra adder
and routing pressure on the hot branch/redirect and writeback-result cones.

hypothesis.motivation:
Recent micro-opt attempts show that this core is sensitive to added control
and pipeline repacking, while the successful changes were local datapath
simplifications. The EX stage currently spells out the same PC-relative
additions in several places for branch targets, direct JAL targets,
sequential pc_next, and JAL/JALR link data. If synthesis does not fully
common those expressions across procedural blocks, it can leave extra adder
and routing pressure on the hot branch/redirect and writeback-result cones.
hypothesis.hypothesis:
Introduce explicit EX-stage shared wires for pc_plus4 and pc_plus_imm, then
use them for branch_target, direct JAL jump_target, resolved fallthrough
pc_next, and jump link rd_wdata. This preserves the current predictor,
redirect, trap, RVFI pc_wdata, and pipeline timing behavior, but encourages
Yosys/nextpnr to map one local PC+4 adder and one local PC+imm adder instead
of duplicating equivalent arithmetic in separate always blocks. The change is
intentionally narrower than the failed ID precompute attempt because it adds
no registers, no wider pipeline bundles, and no new control decisions.

changes.description: Add shared pc_plus4 and pc_plus_imm wires in EX and replace repeated in.pc + 4 / in.pc + imm expressions with those wires, leaving redirect, trap, predictor update, and RVFI semantics unchanged.
Add shared pc_plus4 and pc_plus_imm wires in EX and replace repeated in.pc + 4 / in.pc + imm expressions with those wires, leaving redirect, trap, predictor update, and RVFI semantics unchanged.

lesson: - 2026-05-13 hyp-20260513-001-r13s0 (regression, -9.36%): Explicitly sharing EX-stage pc_plus4/pc_plus_imm adders can hurt because synthesis likely already commoned simple PC additions, while named shared wires increase fanout/routing across redirect, fallthrough, and jump-link cones.

title: Fetch replay latch
motivation: The current front end discards an instruction-memory response whenever the back end is stalled by dmem or DIV/REM, because the PC is held and the IF/ID payload is not a registered valid-ready boundary. In the randomized CoreMark mode, this means an instruction that was available during a back-end stall may need to be refetched and can then be delayed by a later independent imem stall. Prior fetch queues regressed because they advanced PC and added queue control; a one-entry replay latch only captures the current PC response and should be much smaller.

hypothesis.hypothesis: Add a single-entry fetch replay latch in if_stage that records pc, predicted_pc_next, and instr when the pipeline is stalled behind IF but io_imemReady is high. When the back-end stall clears, ID consumes the latched instruction even if io_imemReady is low that cycle, and the PC advances exactly once; redirects and flushes invalidate the latch. This structurally overlaps random instruction-memory availability with dmem/div stalls without adding fetch-ahead policy, branch predictor entries, memory reordering, or nret changes, so it should recover some CoreMark iterations per cycle with less Fmax risk than the failed two-entry skid buffer.

changes.description: Add a one-entry replay latch for the current PC's fetched instruction, consume it before live imem data, and invalidate it on redirect/reset/flush.
Replace raw imem_ready front-end blocking with an effective fetch-available signal so a replayed instruction can advance while a live imem response is stalled.
Wire io_imemReady into if_stage replay capture and route the IF fetch-available/consume signal into hazard control without changing the nret=1 RVFI port shape.

title: Predecessor-keyed lookahead predictor
motivation:
The only predictor variant that improved this core is the current one-entry
registered lookahead predictor. Follow-up attempts regressed when they added
IF lookup entries, redirect-path prearming, candidate promotion, carried
validation bits, or update-policy changes. The remaining useful predictor
angle is therefore to keep the proven CPI behavior while shaving the arming
logic from the front-end timing cone.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Change the one-entry lookahead predictor to store the sequential predecessor
PC of the learned hot control-flow instruction instead of the control-flow
instruction PC itself. IF can then prearm with a direct `pred_prev_pc == pc`
comparison while fetching the predecessor, rather than comparing the stored
branch PC against `pc + 4`. The fetched next PC and predicted target behavior
stay equivalent for sequential arrivals, but the `pc+4` adder output no longer
feeds the predictor tag compare fanout. This preserves the successful single
registered armed-target next-PC mux and current backward-branch plus direct-JAL
training policy while targeting a small Fmax improvement with no intended CPI
loss.

changes.description: Store pred_update_pc - 4 as the predictor key and prearm from a direct predecessor-PC comparison against the current fetch PC, keeping the existing single armed target mux and update policy.

lesson: - 2026-05-13 hyp-20260513-003-r13s2 (regression, -19.03%): Predecessor-keying the one-entry lookahead predictor regressed because the existing `seq_pc` adder fanout was not the binding IF cost, while `pred_update_pc - 4` retagging and changed compare placement perturbed the proven registered-target path.

title: Shared ALU barrel shifter
motivation:
Recent micro-opt attempts show that this core is most likely to improve when
an always-active datapath is simplified without adding control, predictor
state, wider pipeline structs, or new stalls. The current ALU spells SLL,
SRL, and SRA as three separate variable-shift expressions in the common EX
result mux. CoreMark uses shift-heavy CRC/state code, so the shifter network
can matter for both LUT/routing pressure and EX-stage Fmax.

hypothesis.motivation:
Recent micro-opt attempts show that this core is most likely to improve when

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Replace the separate SLL/SRL/SRA expressions in alu.sv with one explicit
shared 32-bit barrel-shifter datapath. Use bit reversal on the input/output
for SLL, use the normal operand order for right shifts, and drive the stage
fill bit from a[31] only for SRA. The ALU result case then selects the single
precomputed shift_result for all three shift ops. This preserves exact RV32IM
semantics and CPI while giving Yosys a clearer one-shifter implementation
instead of three independently inferred variable shifters.

changes.description: Replace the independent SLL, SRL, and SRA variable-shift expressions with a single shared barrel-shifter datapath using reversible input/output wiring and a selectable arithmetic fill bit.
Add focused shift edge vectors for shamt 0/1/31, high-bit operands, and SLL/SRL/SRA equivalence to guard the shared shifter rewrite.

lesson: - 2026-05-13 hyp-20260513-001-r14s0 (regression, -27.11%): Manually sharing SLL/SRL/SRA through bit-reversal and staged barrel muxes can hurt EX timing because synthesis likely already maps native variable shifts well, while the explicit network adds op-dependent fill/reversal fanout on the hot ALU result path.

title: One-cycle registered multiply sidecar
motivation:
The current EX stage still carries three real 32x32 multiply products through
the common combinational ALU result path. Prior high-half-only and long-latency
all-MUL side units regressed: the former left low-half MUL on the hot path,
while the latter paid too much CoreMark CPI on ordinary MUL instructions.
A narrower structural split can remove multiplier logic from the normal
integer/branch/load/store cone while keeping multiply latency to one extra
cycle instead of an iterative stall.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a registered one-cycle multiply sidecar in EX for MUL, MULH, MULHU, and
MULHSU. On a real M-extension multiply, EX captures the forwarded operands
and opcode into a small mul_unit, emits a single bubble into EX/MEM, then
consumes the registered result on the next cycle using the existing ex_busy
stall path. In non-ALTOPS builds, alu.sv no longer computes real multiplier
products; in RISCV_FORMAL_ALTOPS builds, keep the current algebraic stand-ins
in the ALU so formal remains tractable. This should improve Fmax by removing
multiplier fanout and wide M-result muxing from the common EX cone while
limiting CoreMark IPC loss to one cycle per multiply.

changes.description: Remove real non-ALTOPS MUL/MULH/MULHU/MULHSU product logic from the normal ALU path while preserving ALTOPS substitutions.
Add a small registered one-cycle RV32M multiply sidecar that computes all four multiply variants with RV32IM-correct signedness.
Detect multiply operations, launch the sidecar, emit one EX/MEM bubble while the registered result is produced, and write the sidecar result into the retiring instruction.

title: Sticky loop lookahead predictor
motivation:
The only predictor variant that improved this core is the current one-entry
registered lookahead predictor, because IF consumes only prearmed registered
state. Follow-up attempts regressed when they added IF lookup entries,
redirect-path prearming, candidate promotion, carried validation bits, or
broad update-policy changes. The remaining promising predictor angle is to
preserve the proven fetch datapath while making the single entry less likely
to be overwritten by lower-value direct jumps immediately after it proves
useful for a hot loop branch.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a small update-admission guard to the existing one-entry lookahead
predictor: remember when the active entry was a backward conditional branch
and was just consumed as an armed prediction, and for a short window refuse
to replace that active loop entry with a different forward direct JAL. Taken
backward branches still update immediately, direct JALs still refresh their
own entry and can fill an empty/non-loop entry, and the IF-side lookup remains
exactly one registered prearm compare feeding one registered target mux. This
should reduce one-entry thrash between hot CoreMark loop backs and nearby
calls without repeating the failed all-JAL filtering or two-entry IF lookup
experiments.

changes.description: Track whether the active predictor entry is a backward-branch loop entry, mark it recently used when its armed prediction is consumed, and gate replacement by different direct-JAL updates while keeping the IF lookup and next-PC mux one-entry/registered.

Expose whether each predictor update came from a taken backward conditional branch or a direct JAL so if_stage can apply loop-entry replacement priority without changing redirect, RVFI pc_next, or trap behavior.
Wire the predictor update class signal from EX to IF alongside the existing update valid, PC, and target signals.

title: Isolate MEM word-load fast path
motivation:
The strongest successful micro-optimization simplified the MEM byte-lane
datapath, while most broader control, forwarding, ALU, and predictor changes
regressed. The current MEM stage still computes byte and halfword extraction
logic from dmem_rdata on every cycle, even though CoreMark's hottest memory
operations are likely aligned word loads and stores. Keeping the proven case
mux discipline but isolating the word-load path may trim dmem_rdata fanout
and preserve Fmax without changing CPI or pipeline control.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Restructure mem_stage load-data generation so aligned word loads take a
direct dmem_rdata path, while byte and halfword lane extraction is only in
the narrow-width arms. Leave the existing byte-mask generation,
misalignment trap detection, dmem gating, RVFI aligned-memory reporting, and
MEM/WB timing unchanged. This should give synthesis a cleaner fast path for
the common LW case and avoid reintroducing the failed halfword special-case
or EX precompute control costs.

title: Small instruction hit cache
motivation:
The tournament CoreMark mode injects roughly 22% random instruction-memory
backpressure, and the current front end stalls the whole pipeline whenever
io_imemReady is low. Prior fetch skid and replay latches only tried to
overlap a single available response with backend stalls; they did not exploit
CoreMark's repeated execution of a small hot code footprint. A tiny internal
instruction cache can convert recurrent loop-body fetches into local hits and
avoid many exposed iStall cycles without changing retirement width or the
branch predictor policy.

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a small direct-mapped instruction word cache inside the IF path with
valid, tag, and 32-bit instruction arrays indexed by pc[6:2] or a similarly
small power-of-two slice. On a cache hit, IF should present the cached
instruction and assert an internal fetch-ready signal even when io_imemReady
is low; on a miss, it should use the existing external imem response and fill
the entry when io_imemReady is high, preserving fill-through so a ready miss
has no extra latency. The hazard unit should stall on this internal
fetch-ready signal rather than raw io_imemReady, while redirects and reset
keep the cache contents coherent enough for the static benchmark image. This
structural decoupling targets CoreMark iterations/cycle directly by removing
repeated instruction-port stalls from hot loops, with the main risk being the
added IF tag compare on Fmax.

Wire io_imemReady into IF, expose the IF cache hit/miss ready signal, and pass that signal to the hazard unit without changing the nret=1 RVFI port set.
Treat instruction fetch as stalled only when the IF-stage cache/memory fetch-ready signal is low, leaving load-use, dmem, redirect, and divide stall priorities unchanged.

title: Loop-exit aware lookahead predictor
motivation:
The current one-entry registered lookahead predictor is the only predictor
variant that has improved this core because the fetch next-PC mux consumes
only registered armed state. It still predicts every learned backward loop
branch as taken, so fixed-trip CoreMark loops pay one full EX-corrected
mispredict at each loop exit. Avoiding those exit flushes targets remaining
branch CPI while preserving the successful no-current-PC-lookup timing shape.

hypothesis.motivation:

variant that has improved this core because the fetch next-PC mux consumes

hypothesis.hypothesis:
Add a tiny loop-exit counter overlay to the existing one-entry lookahead
predictor. Keep the same active PC/target entry, the same single sequential
lookahead prearm point, and the same registered armed-target next-PC mux, but
also learn the observed taken-count for the active backward branch when that
branch eventually falls through. On later visits, suppress arming for exactly
the learned exit iteration so the fetch stream naturally continues to pc+4;
EX still validates predicted_pc_next and repairs any stale or irregular loop
behavior. This should recover CoreMark loop-exit bubbles without adding a
second IF lookup entry, redirect prearming, candidate promotion, or direct-JAL
filtering, all of which have regressed previously.

Provide predictor update information for both taken backward branches and the not-taken fall-through of the active loop branch so IF can learn and refresh the loop trip count without changing RVFI pc_next or redirect correction semantics.
Wire the added loop-outcome predictor update signals between EX and IF while preserving the nret=1 top-level IO contract and existing stall/flush priorities.
